2024 SCC 12 [2024] 2 SCR 156 [2024] 2 S.C.R. 156
Supreme Court of Canada 2024
2024-04-18
Wagner C.J. Karakatsanis J. Côté J. Rowe J. Martin J. Kasirer J. Jamal J.

I. Introduction

1

This appeal raises the issue of whether warrantless aerial thermal imaging of a residence constitutes a "search" within the meaning of s. 8 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.

2

The appellant operated a small-scale cannabis grow operation in the basement of a rented residence. RCMP officers, acting on an anonymous tip, conducted a flyover of the property using a FLIR camera and observed heat-emission patterns consistent with an indoor grow operation.

II. Facts and Procedural History

3

The flyover took place on the night of February 11, 2022. No warrant was sought prior to the use of the FLIR camera. The resulting imagery was then used as one of the grounds for a search warrant under s. 11 of the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act.

4

At trial, the appellant moved to exclude the evidence obtained pursuant to the warrant on the basis that the underlying FLIR observation was itself an unreasonable search.

5

The trial judge dismissed the application, holding that the FLIR observation revealed only ambient heat radiating from the exterior of the building and did not penetrate the home or reveal "intimate details" of the appellant's lifestyle.

III. Analysis

A. The Reasonable Expectation of Privacy

6

The threshold question under s. 8 is whether the appellant had a reasonable expectation of privacy in the heat-emission information observed by police. As this Court has repeatedly affirmed, the inquiry is normative rather than purely descriptive.

7

We hold that the appellant did have a reasonable expectation of privacy in the heat-emission information. The Crown's submission that the heat had "escaped" the home and was therefore in the public domain conflates physical and informational privacy.

B. The Section 8 Standard

8

A warrantless search is prima facie unreasonable. The burden rests on the Crown to establish that the search was authorised by law, that the authorising law itself was reasonable, and that the manner in which the search was carried out was reasonable: R. v. Collins, [1987] 1 S.C.R. 265.

9

No statutory authority was advanced for the FLIR observation. The Crown relied solely on common law plain-view doctrine. We conclude that doctrine has no application to technologically enhanced sensing of areas that would otherwise be private.

C. Section 24(2)

10

Turning to the question of exclusion under s. 24(2) of the Charter, the Grant framework requires the Court to balance three lines of inquiry: the seriousness of the Charter-infringing state conduct, the impact of the breach on the Charter-protected interests of the accused, and society's interest in the adjudication of the case on its merits.

11

The state conduct here was deliberate and arose in a context in which the legal landscape was settled. The breach significantly impacted the appellant's residential privacy interest. While the public interest in adjudicating drug offences is real, it does not outweigh the first two factors on the facts of this case.

IV. Disposition

12

The appeal is allowed. The conviction is set aside. We order a new trial at which the impugned evidence is to be excluded.
